summary: Gruber argues that the iPod FireWire cable controversy revealed a deeper anxiety among Mac users: the fear that the iPod's success will come at the Mac's expense. He traces how Apple's identity became conflated with the Mac during its years as a one-product company, and explains that the Mac's loyal but static user base of 10-20 million has sustained profits without delivering growth. The iPod's explosive success means the Mac is now one division among several rather than the whole company, which some users experience as abandonment. Gruber dismisses this fear as irrational, noting the Mac remains profitable, and dismantles the argument that Apple should have kept the iPod FireWire-only to boost that standard, pointing out the iPod only became a phenomenon after adding USB support.
key_points: Mac users conflate Apple with the Mac, so iPod success feels like Mac abandonment rather than company growth
Mac OS X sustained the Mac as profitable but never grew the installed base beyond the long-claimed 25 million users
The iPod became a mass-market phenomenon only after adding USB 2.0 support — FireWire-only iPods sold one million in 18 months versus one million in six months after USB
Telling Apple to 'become a consumer electronics company' without specifying products is like telling someone to 'become a millionaire'
Apple's obligation is to shareholders, not to subsidize FireWire adoption through iPod bundling decisions
money_quotes: They say they love Apple, but what they love is the Mac.
If you take it personally that the Mac has perhaps slipped to Apple's second-most-profitable division, you're weird.
Saying that Apple should 'become a consumer electronics company' is like telling someone they should 'become a millionaire'.
This is completely backwards, confusing the horse for the wagon.
People aren't buying new computers just to use with their iPods — they're buying iPods to use with their existing computers.
FireWire, much like the Macintosh, is neither a failure nor a phenomenon. It is simply successful in a nice-sized niche, but not the entire market.
Apple's interest is in selling iPods, not raising the average quality of de facto standard data-transfer buses.
Great means growth, and growth is what drives a stock price up.
key_insight: The iPod's success doesn't diminish the Mac — it transforms Apple from a one-platform company into a multi-division one, and users who conflate their loyalty to the Mac with loyalty to Apple are misreading the situation.
